[01:49:03] again. A control system is basically any
[01:49:05] mechanism that maintains balance between
[01:49:07] where you are and where you're trying to
[01:49:08] be. It compares your current state to a
[01:49:11] desired goal and makes corrections to
[01:49:13] stay on track. This is how thermostats,
[01:49:15] for example, work and regulate
[01:49:16] temperature. How self-driving cars stay
[01:49:18] in lane and how healthy bodies maintain
[01:49:21] homeostasis. The system constantly runs
[01:49:23] a loop. It takes input, it compares it
[01:49:25] to the target and adjusts the output and
[01:49:27] then repeats. So, for example, if your
[01:49:29] room drops below 21° C or whatever it is
[01:49:33] in Fahrenheit and your thermostat is set
[01:49:35] to 22° C, it turns the heat on. That's a
[01:49:39] control system in action. So, what most
[01:49:41] people don't realize is that your entire
[01:49:43] life runs on these loops. And when the
[01:49:44] loop is broken or badly wired, you
[01:49:46] spiral out of control without even
[01:49:48] noticing. This is where most people miss
[01:49:50] it. They don't realize that every result
[01:49:52] they're experiencing, good or bad, isn't
[01:49:54] personal. It's just a system. It's

[01:50:00] stagnation, it's not because you're lazy

[01:50:05] system is how anything stable maintains

[01:50:13] thermostats work. And that's how your
[01:50:14] body regulates temperature as well. And
[01:50:17] that's how your brain is supposed to
[01:50:18] regulate success. Input, feedback,

[15:07:23] your brain doesn't see reality as it is.
[15:07:25] It sees it as it wants it to be or as

[15:07:37] mental glitches, flaws in perception,
[15:07:39] judgment, and memory. They're shortcuts
[15:07:42] gone sideways. They're your brain trying
[15:07:44] to help you survive, but instead making
[15:07:46] you lie to yourself without even
[15:07:48] realizing it. If you don't learn to spot
[15:07:50] them, they will sabotage every decision
[15:07:53] you make. So, this isn't theory. Biases
[15:07:57] are why you chase the wrong people,
[15:07:59] double down on bad investments, ignore
[15:08:01] red flags, misjudge others, and stay
[15:08:04] stuck in fake problems, and tell
[15:08:06] yourself convincing stories that aren't
[15:08:08] remotely true. They corrupt the entire
[15:08:11] system. It doesn't matter how good your
[15:08:12] logic is. If your inputs are actually
[15:08:14] distorted, if your perception is broken,
[15:08:17] your conclusions are most likely broken,
[15:08:19] too. So, for example, you see a tweet
[15:08:22] from someone with a 100,000 followers
[15:08:24] and assume they must be right. That's
[15:08:26] actually authority bias. You dismiss an
[15:08:29] opportunity because it didn't work last
[15:08:31] time. That's recency bias. You clink to
[15:08:33] a bad idea because you've already spent
[15:08:35] 3 months on it. That's the sunk cost
[15:08:37] fallacy. These glitches aren't rare.
[15:08:40] They're happening all the time without
[15:08:41] you even realizing, and they're subtle,
[15:08:43] and you can't brute force your way past
[15:08:45] them. You need awareness, precision, and
[15:08:48] constant recalibration. But biases
[15:08:51] aren't necessarily evil. They evolved
[15:08:53] for a reason. Your brain had to make
[15:08:55] fast judgments to survive. Back when you
[15:08:57] were being chased by predators or trying
[15:08:59] not to eat poisonous berries, shortcuts
[15:09:02] made sense. They still do sometimes, but
[15:09:04] in today's world, they usually make you
[15:09:06] weak, reactive, and blind. That's not
[15:09:08] survival. That's self- sabotage. Your
[15:09:11] brain trades accuracy for efficiency
[15:09:14] because thinking is actually hard. It
[15:09:16] will give you a fast answer that feels
[15:09:18] true over a slow one that actually is
[15:09:21] true. It doesn't care about truth. It
[15:09:23] cares about safety. That's why most
[15:09:25] biases feel emotionally comfortable.
[15:09:27] They confirm your beliefs, soothe your
[15:09:29] fears, or reinforce your ego. Now, the
[15:09:32] problem isn't just that biases exist.
[15:09:34] It's that you don't know when they're
[15:09:36] happening. That's why they're dangerous.
[15:09:38] They don't announce themselves. They
[15:09:39] don't ask permission. They just

[15:09:45] even realize it. The only defense is
[15:09:47] awareness. Ruthless structured
[15:09:50] awareness. Most people only catch their
[15:09:52] biases in hindsight, if at all. You need
[15:09:54] to get ahead of them. Know the patterns.
[15:09:56] Know when you're most vulnerable and
[15:09:58] know which ones hit you hardest. Build
[15:10:01] your own internal trip wires. And there
[15:10:03] are dozens of cognitive biases, but some
[15:10:05] show up more than others, especially in
[15:10:08] business, relationships, and decision-m.
[15:10:10] These are the ones you need to tattoo on
[15:10:12] your brain and start catching in the
[15:10:14] wild. One of them is confirmation bias.
[15:10:17] You only look for evidence that proves
[15:10:18] what you already believe and you ignore,
[15:10:21] dismiss or rationalize anything that
[15:10:23] actually challenges it. And this is the

[15:10:27] you don't question it daily, you become
[15:10:29] a prisoner of your own assumptions.
[15:10:31] Another one is the sunk cost fallacy.
[15:10:34] You keep investing time, energy, or
[15:10:36] money into something just because you've
[15:10:38] already put a lot in. Even when it's
[15:10:40] clearly failing, you forget that the
[15:10:42] past is gone and that doubling down
[15:10:44] doesn't recover your losses. It deepens
[15:10:46] them. Another one is anchoring. Your
[15:10:49] brain fixates on the first piece of
[15:10:51] information it sees, and every judgment

[15:10:56] Whether it's a price, an idea, or a
[15:10:58] first impression, you stop seeing
[15:11:00] clearly after the first data point. This
[15:11:02] is used extensively into in supermarkets
[15:11:06] for example. Now the next one is
[15:11:08] availability bias. If it's easier to
[15:11:10] remember, you assume it must be more
[15:11:12] likely, more common, or more true. This
[15:11:14] makes you overestimate risks that are
[15:11:16] vivid and underestimate risks that are
[15:11:18] invisible. Fear loves this one. The next
[15:11:21] one is the Dunning Krueger effect, which
[15:11:24] is incompetent people overestimate their
[15:11:26] skill. Experts underestimate theirs. The
[15:11:29] less you know, the more confident you
[15:11:31] feel. The more you learn, the humbler
[15:11:33] you become. If you're feeling too sure,
[15:11:35] you might be deep in this one. Now, you
[15:11:38] can't eliminate bias completely, but you

[15:22:18] well, your mind resists changing
